scenario_results.reason: Charter guidance stayed inside architecture-governance scope and produced clear decision rights, scope boundaries, and review cadence.
Standards lifecycle guidance defined waiver controls, expiration, and escalation without drifting into migration architecture ownership.
Conformance review required evidence, highlighted principle and guardrail misalignment, and avoided final solution-design ownership.
Exception handling stayed evidence-based, time-bound, and accountable while preserving governance rather than implementation work.
Prioritization used business capability impact, risk, and evidence weakness instead of generic architecture commentary.
Merger integration guidance focused on governance sequencing, decision rights, and traceability rather than target-state architecture design.
Commercial overlap analysis framed architecture-governance criteria and routed detailed licensing and sourcing work to adjacent specialists.
Traceability review called out evidence lineage gaps and confidence limits clearly enough for defensible governance review.
Detailed solution design and implementation ownership were rejected cleanly with appropriate routing guidance.
Legal interpretation and procurement negotiation requests were rejected while preserving only narrow architecture-governance observations.
Low-confidence behavior requested the minimum missing architecture-governance baseline and refused to invent unsupported structure.
Fallback behavior preserved the local reviewed baseline and kept the validated claim within repository evidence.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against issue #132 requirements, including governance analysis, commercial reasoning, evidence and traceability quality, risk-based prioritization, low-confidence escalation, out-of-scope rejection, and shared-meta-agent fallback behavior.
No unreviewed regressions remain and human verification is recorded in readiness evidence.
